How do you ensure that the aeronautical information that you provide for promulgation in the AIP and elsewhere meets the required accuracies? b. When was the last time such information was revised? c. Did it meet the accuracy requirements? ii. Control of Access a. Where are the procedures for allowing appropriate access on airside? b. How do you measure the effectiveness of the procedures for controlling access to airside? iii. Inspection of the Movement Area a. How do you measure the runway precipitation on your runways? b. What do you do if there is more than 25% of the runway covered with water over 3 mm in depth? c. What do you do if instead of 25%, it is 50% coverage? d. When was the last time you measured runway precipitation and what were the results? e. What did you do with the results? Did that follow SOP? f. When was the last scheduled check on PAPIs conducted? g. What were the results? h. Show me please.

iv. Maintenance of the Movement area a. Is this maintenance procedures being implemented? b. If not, why not? c. If yes, is the objective being met? d. When was the last and the next activity scheduled? e. How and where are the procedures spelt out? f. How is it coordinated with users and other stakeholders? v. Obstacle a. When was the last survey undertaken? b. Did it show any change or additional obstructions in the Type B chart area that penetrated the performance surface? c. If so, whether the chart was revised. If yes, when? d. What does your organization do if there is a penetration of the obstacle control surfaces? e. Can you please show me the procedures please? vi. Low Visibility Procedures a. How effective is/was the procedure during the most recent period of low visibility conditions? b. How do you measure the effectiveness? viii. Apron Safety Management a. What is the greatest safety concern on the apron? b. How are you trying to resolve apron management and apron operating procedures? c. How effective is it? d. Are the outcomes consistent with the agreed safety targets? ix. Vehicle on the Movement Area a. Which department in your organization has the responsibility for overall control of vehicles on the apron? b. How is control exercised? c. How do you determine if it is effective enough? d. If not considered effective enough, what action is being taken? x. Aerodrome Emergency Planning a. What are the procedures in place for:

I) Coordinating with ATC? II) AOCC operations during an AEP exercise or actual emergency? xii. Wildlife Hazard Management a. What is the greatest risk to safety in this context? b. How do you get the result of a wildlife survey? c. If not from a wildlife survey or extensive pre-knowledge, how have you made that judgement? d. Are things changing? e. How do you know that? xiii. Aerodrome Emergency Plan a. When was the plan last tested? b. If not in recent times, how do you know if it is still effective and adequate? xiv. Dangerous Goods a. Who are the personnel trained in handling hazardous materials? b. Do you have the training records for them? I.If not, write down a finding.

II.If yes, show me please. c. When was the last refresher course conducted? d. Do any other staff members get involved with hazardous materials? If yes, have they had training and if not why? xv. Protection of Sites for Radar, Navigation Aids and Meteoroidal Equipment a. Do you know what the effects would be on the aircraft using navigational aids if the critical/sensitive areas are infringed? b. How do you ensure that these areas are not infringed? c. To what extent is the mechanism effective? d. How do you know that? xvi.

What processes do you have for occurrence reporting? b. What do you do with the reports? c. Are the person reporting the occurrence being advised of the follow up action? d. If not, why not? xvii. a. Are there other airport SOPs for specific operations? b. If so, may I see them please.

A.2 Aerodrome Emergency Plan i. a. Who chairs your Aerodrome Emergency Plan Coordination Committee and when did it last meet? b. What actions were agreed during the last meeting? c. Have these actions been carried out? ii.

a.When was the last aerodrome emergency exercise conducted? b.Whether any shortcoming/deficiencies were noticed/observed during the conduct of the exercise? c.If yes, were the corrective actions taken? d.If not, why not? iii.

a.Who is nominated as on-scene commander? b.Is that person/post available on duty today? iv. a.What is the plan at your aerodrome for communication within the AEP? v. a. How do you evaluate the outcomes from the exercises? b. How is the output communicated to the interested parties? A.3 Safety Committees and Safety Teams i.

a. What are the terms of reference for the Airport Safety Committee? b. Is the membership and role in line with the guidelines? ii. a.What outputs are currently being implemented by the runway safety team? iii.

a.What outputs are currently being implemented by the Apron Safety Team? A.4 Runway and Environs i. a.What is the runway inspection regime? b.What was the last maintenance action undertaken on the runway? c.What preventive maintenance do you undertake? d.How do you stabilize the surface of the shoulders to minimize the risk of engine ingestion damage? ii. Slope a.Whether the runway slopes are in compliance with GACAR Part 139? b.How do you check the slope? c.If slopes are not in compliance with GACAR Part 139, do you have an exemption? d.Do you ask for operational feedback from aircraft operators on the runway condition? e.If so, how do you do this? iii. Sand on runway a.During heavy dust storms, do the runways get covered with sand? b.How do you determine this? c.What action/s do you take and is there a program for corrective action? iv. Runway Strip a.If we go out to inspect the runway strip, will they be fully compliant with no exposed duct, soil heaps, uneven ground or area of substandard bearing strength? v. Runway Separation a.Are the runway separation distances compatible with the requirement of regulation? vi. Runway End Safety Area (RESA) a.How have you determined that your RESA is sufficient in size? vii. Critical and Sensitive Area a.How do you ensure that the ILS critical and sensitive areas are not infringed? A.5 Taxiways and Interfaces i.

a.What is the taxiway inspection regime? b.What was the last maintenance action undertaken on the taxiways? And on which taxiways? c.What preventative maintenance do you undertake? ii. Taxiway bearing strength a.Do you have any taxiway where the movement takes place for the aircraft of higher code than the one for which it is designed? b.If so, have you undertaken the aerodrome compatibility and an assessment of the risk? iii. Taxiway separation a.Are the taxiway separation standards compatible with the aircraft codes using the runway? b.If not, what risk control measures do you have in place? iv. Taxiway strip a.How do you stabilize the surface of the strips to minimize the risk of engine ingestion damage? v. Slope of taxiway a.Are any of the taxiway slopes non-compliant? Are they considered to be a hazard? b.How did you make this judgement? vi. Precipitation of water a.Do you have a taxiway that has standing water on the surface during heavy or prolonged rainfall? b.If so, has the drainage been assessed for adequacy? vii. Occurrence reported a.Have you had any occurrence reported, verbally or in writing, involving the apron to runway route designations during the last year? b.If so, what corrective actions have been taken? viii. Hot Spot a.Does your hot spot chart reflect any issue raised at the item (vii) above?

A.6 APRON AREAS

i. a.What is the apron surface inspection regime? b.What was the last maintenance action undertaken on the apron? c.What preventive program do you undertake for the apron? ii. Foreign Object Debris a.Is the presence of FOD on the apron increasing or decreasing? b.How do you know? iii. Markings a.When were the apron markings last repainted? b.Are these markings adequate at present? c.Do you have plans to repaint these markings? If yes, when? iv. Apron Floodlighting a.Does the apron floodlighting create any distraction to air traffic controllers or to the pilots on the maneuvering area? b.Have you had any occurrence reported, verbally or in writing involving apron floodlighting like glare or insufficient illumination? c.If yes, what action has been taken? v. Apron Safety Lines a. Does the airport have ever encountered a problem with the equipment parked outside the designated areas? b. If yes, what action is taken? c. If no problem is encountered, can we go to the apron areas now? vi. Signs a.Are there mandatory and information signs available which are associated with the apron area or at the taxiway/apron interface? b.If yes, are they fully compliant? c.If no, should there be any? vii. Clearance distances on aircraft stands a.Are the aircraft stand clearances compliant with the regulations? b.If yes, how have you determined that? c.If not, what corrective action has been taken? viii. Coordination a.Which organization/department has an overall coordination role on the apron? b.How is that coordination applied in order that close liaison is achieved between ATS, the airline and the airport?

A.7 VISUAL NAVIGATIONAL AIDS

i. a.When were the PAPI units last checked? b.How is this done? c.Is that in accordance with the manufacturer’s maintenance schedule? d.What was the result? e.Can you show me the record of maintenance and any corrective actions taken? ii. Flight check a.When was the last flight check of the visual aids conducted? b.What were the findings of the flight check? c.Did the flight check indicate any ambiguity or finding? d.If needed, whether the corrective action has been taken or not? e.Can you please show me all reports? iii. Photometric and serviceability requirement (for CAT II and III) a.Do the runway and approach lighting systems meet the photometric and serviceability requirements? b.If yes, how do you measure it? iv. Runway Markings a.When were the runway markings last painted? b.Are these markings adequate at present? c.Do you have any plans to repaint the markings? v. Obstruction Lights a.Are there any superfluous obstruction lights that may be contributing to light pollution and distraction to ATC or pilots? vi. Unserviceable Areas a.What is the policy and method for marking and lighting of unserviceable areas? b.Is it effective? c.How do you ensure its effectiveness?

A.8 MOVEMENT AREA

i. a.Does your Surface Movement and Guidance Control System (SMGCS) meet the guidance as provided in

GACAR §139.915?

b.If not, what action is being taken? c.If no action has been taken, whether any of the non-adherence has been featured in the identified “Hot Spots”? ii. Obliteration of unused marking a.What is your process for confirming that obliteration of the unused markings or repainting of the movement area markings have been undertaken as planned? b.In relation to the aircraft movements, at what time is the action taken to ensure compliance? c.Can you demonstrate as to when the last removal/repainting was done? iii. Drawings (Markings) a.Do you have a drawing indicating the location of the mandatory/ information markings? b.How often do you review the appropriateness of these markings? iv. Drawing (Sign Boards) a.Please show me the drawing showing the Mandatory and Information Sign Boards.

b.How often do you review the appropriateness of these sign boards? v. Light output a.How do you measure the light output to ensure that the air/ground lighting meets the requirement/specification? vi. Obstacle lights a.How do you ensure that the obstruction/obstacle lighting is still appropriate and does not contribute to confusion to ATC or pilot? vii. SMGCS a.Does your SMGCS for “vehicular use” meet the guidance as provided in GACAR 139.915? If not, what action is being taken? b.If none, has there been any exercise conducted to determine the areas that present the greatest risk of collision by a vehicle with an aircraft of a runway incursion by a vehicle?

A.9 OBSTACLE ENVIRONMENT

i. a.Is the Type B chart in line with the current Obstacle environment? b.Has there been a full survey undertaken? If yes, when? If no, why not? c.If so, has the data shown in the survey been compared with the proposed runway coding, orientation and landing aid category? d.If so, would the site be compliant in terms of the obstacle environment and obstacle limitation surface? e.Are there any obstacles that infringe the obstacle clearance surface? f.If so, are they on agreed non-compliance and are there operational restrictions or other mitigation measures in place?

A.10 OTHER OPERATIONAL AREAS/FEATURES

i. a. How secure is the boundary wall/fence? b. Can you give evidence of this? ii. a.What are the clearances of the boundary wall from operational areas and visual/electronic aids?

A.11 WILDLIFE MANAGEMENET

i. a. How would you detect a change in bird flight paths on the aerodrome? ii. a.How do you measure the effectiveness of methods used to control and manage wildlife hazards? iii. a.How do you know if you have enough skilled and trained manpower and other resources for managing the wildlife management? b.If services of the contractor are used for this purpose, have you audited their manpower resources, procedures and verified effectiveness of the control?

A.12 RESCUE AND FIRE FIGHTING

i. a.Does the RFF category meet the requirement for the class of aircraft using the airport in terms of fuselage length and movement numbers? ii. a.How many vehicles are online? b.Is that in compliance? iii.

a.How do you ensure that the extinguishing agents remain effective as intended? Records are required. b.Where are they stored? And how do you ensure that the storage facility is safe and acceptable? c.Do you have a plan to transfer the extinguishing agents needed to any point in the airport with a reasonable time? How long? iv.

a.Do the Standards Operating Procedures cover all the subjects available in GACAR 139? Review a copy. b.If yes, are all personnel familiar with it? Evidence is required. v. a.How do you ensure that all on duty shifts are performing their tasks 24/7? Evidence is required.

vi. a.Are the training activities conducted as per monthly schedule? Evidence is required. b.Is the annual training accepted by GACA? c.Can you show me the evidence of that by training records? vii. a.Whether RFFS staff completed their training courses as required in GACAR 139? b.If yes, evidence may be furnished.

c.If not, a formal training plan is required. viii. a.What are the water sources for meeting the water requirement for extinguishing the fires, both for initial and sustained response? ix. a.How often are the airside water hydrants tested? Evidence is required.

b.Request to test a hydrant randomly. x. a)Request the latest grid map/chart to ensure that it accurately reflects the airside infrastructure and layout. b)Are all crash gates correctly shown in the grid map? c)Are all RFF vehicles provided with an updated grid map? d)Is the FCC provided with an updated grid map? e)Are PCP & MCP provided with an updated grid map? xi.

a.Are there clear access roads to all crash gates? b.If yes, check randomly. c.If no, what is the procedure to facilitate safe access for ARFF vehicles? d.Are all crash gates regularly checked? Evidence is required.

xii. a.Is the response time test regularly conducted? Evidence is required. b.If the required time is not met, what action is being taken? xiii a.Request the scheduled program for maintaining the fire vehicles? b.Request the RFF vehicles and equipment records.

xiv. a.Request records to ensure that exercises and drills have been conducting?

A.13 MAINTENANCE

i. a. How are human factor principles taken account of in the design and application of the maintenance program? ii. Policy for CAP a.What is the policy for corrective action plan when a portion of the runway shows coefficient of friction characteristics lower than the specified minimum? iii. Light Output a.How are the orientation, outputs and beam spread of the light units of the visual aids measured to determine serviceability status?

A.14 ELECTRICAL POWER SYSTEMS

i. a.Does the switchover time from primary to secondary power meet the requirement? b.When was this tested and what were the results obtained? c.Can you show me the records please? ii. Visual Guidance a.For a runway meant for use in RVR of less than 550 meters, what system is used to ensure that the pilots will not be left without adequate visual guidance should there be a failure of ground power equipment? iii. Stop Bars a.Do you have stop bars at the runway holding positions? How are these stop bars controlled? b.Whether the stop bars are interlocked with the taxiway centerline lights installed beyond the stop bar? c.Is a system for monitoring and controlling the lighting configuration? Can you please explain?

B. ORGANIZATIONAL COMPETENCE

i. a.How does your organization evaluate and determine if it is organizationally competent? b.How is it determined whether this method is effective? Figure 12.21.3.1 – AUDIT TECHNIQUES A. Night Inspection i. Purpose:

To be satisfied that the facilities provided meet the safety requirements for “Night Time Operation”, and that the certificate holders have an effective process in place to: a. Monitor the adequacy and performance of the facilities available for the night time and for Low Visibility Operation (LVO) condition; and b. Determine if the night time and LVP conditions present specific or additional and unidentified areas of risk.

ii. Areas/issues/points to inspect and for audit questions are given below: a. Viewing the overall airside facilities from the ATC tower can be a very effective way of seeing the system as a whole and system interface. A visit to the ATC tower before the inspection may indicate areas to be inspected, such as a new Work-in-Progress (W.I.P.) area that does not look secure from an incursion point of view. A visit to this area may confirm a non-compliance/finding as being significant or not. It is also an opportunity to seek another view/opinion on adequacy.

b. Be familiar with what ATC personnel and pilots need to see in terms of pattern of lights e.g. the minimum number of runway centerline lights for an ILS approach. c. Look for: • “Black Holes” that may present a hazard in the context of runway incursion outcomes; or • Potential situations, particularly involving temporary layouts due to W.I.P that may have introduced confusing or ambiguous situations that could lead a pilot or ATC officer into a wrong interpretation of marking, signage or lighting or a combination of those individual elements, with the potential for putting an aircraft into a position other than that intended.

d. Whilst you may be seeing the situation in good or average visual conditions during surveillance, try to picture it in the minimal conditions that would be permitted in terms of visibility, precipitation, wind conditions and cloud cover.

CONCLUSION:

Findings from this area of inspection may be more about the effectiveness of the facility rather than compliance. B. AEP and RFF i. Purpose: To be satisfied with the basic compliance with the requirements, the inspector needs to be satisfied with:

a. RFF: That the system of the vehicles, extinguishing agents, firefighter’s procedures, management and training is “FIT FOR PURPOSE”. This must consider the operational environment and traffic mix. For example, whether each duty shift has enough trained firefighters in all the tasks that could be expected from them for discharging their functions effectively during that duty shift in normal and abnormal conditions.

b. AEP: Consider those areas where potential latent weaknesses, relating to procedures and equipment for communication, may emerge if one of the lines of defense fails; ii. Areas/issues/points to inspect and for audit questions are given below:

a. More obvious audit style questions that arise from above may need to be formulated to test any statement or assertions given about “FITNESS FOR PURPOSE”. Often these are unclear until the audit has started. For example, on finding out that some of the firefighters are on standby at home or undertaking other secondary tasks, questions about response capability arise.

b. Think as a whole system and not just measurable or countable elements. c. Be prepared to ask the “What if?” questions, for example, what if the handheld radio of a key player in the communication/coordination plan fails during an emergency.

CONCLUSION:

The inspector must ensure the match between the provision of the staff and the demands that may be placed on the system, during normal or abnormal operations especially in the light of the prevailing traffic density and mix of the aircraft fleet, as well as airport environment, including “visibility conditions.”

C. APRON

i. Purpose: To be satisfied that the certificate holders ensure compliance through process/procedural means that the apron areas, including any cargo apron, are operated in a disciplined way in accordance with the SOPs and that they:

a. Have processes in place to monitor all activities; and b. Can take corrective action as and when necessary. ii. Areas/issues/points to inspect and for audit questions are given below: a. Overall system compliance with GACARs.

b. Confirm that any W.I.P./unserviceable areas are correctly marked, lit, promulgated and that clearances comply with GACARs and SOPs /policies. c. That there are SOPs for all vehicular movements and that stakeholders, particularly the aircraft operators, adhere to them do this by physically observing a turnaround as a sample after having studied the SOPs.

CONCLUSION:

The inspector must ensure the assurances from the certificate holder about the system compliance. The inspector must see evidence of coordinated and disciplined activity taking place on the aircraft stands while entering on to the stands and for the preparation for aircraft turnaround.

D. WILDLIFE MANAGEMENT

i. Purpose: To address the high-profile safety concern caused by wildlife to generate a catastrophic outcome to an aircraft on take-off or landing. ii. Areas/issues/points to inspect and for audit questions:

a. Applicants/Certificate holders’ processes to collect, analyze and act on data. b. Applicants/Certificate holders’ knowledge of the bird strike threats. c. Applicants/Certificate holders’ wildlife management program to contain threats, including:

• Ensuring security of the airport boundary wall or fences. • Coordination with stakeholders. • Bird strike control methods, including: o Grass maintenance program; o Continual review and provision of adequate resources;

o Monitoring processes for adherence to procedures and follow up/corrective actions; o Management of garbage areas; o Recording and analyzing strikes and promulgating data.

CONCLUSION:

The inspector needs to be satisfied with the threat to the system level.

E. SURFACE MOVEMENT GUIDANCE AND CONTROL SYSTEM (SMGCS)

i. Purpose: To confirm through physical inspection, audit and observation techniques that the aerodrome maintains and operates as planned, a system that meets the need of (aircraft) operational objectives, e.g. CAT II and III landing and takeoff in <400 m RVR. The system comprises of elements that include hardware (equipment), software (procedures, training) and life ware (people), as described in the context of Human Factors.

ii. Areas/issues/points to inspect and for audit questions: a. Does the system meet the guidance given below, as prescribed in GACAR Part 137,138 and 139 for: • Equipment: Ground lighting and signage. • Other guidance provision markings and lights • Procedures.

b. Specialist driver and operative training for those permitted to drive on airside during Low Visibility Operation (LVO).

CONCLUSION:

The inspector needs to plan the inspection of this area with specific focus on the elements of the system including procedures and operative training, as well as physical provision.

F. SURFACE MOVEMENT GUIDANCE AND CONTROL SYSTEM (SMGCS)

i. Interface Purpose: Apron management service is a service provided to regulate the activities and movement of aircraft and vehicles on an apron. Depending upon volume of traffic and operating conditions, an appropriate Apron Management Service is provided by an ATS unit, aerodrome operating authority or by cooperative combination of these.

When the aerodrome control tower partially participates in the Apron Management service, the inspector is obligated to see that the procedures are established and followed to facilitate the orderly transition of functions between the apron management unit and the aerodrome control tower.

ii. Salient points/issues/areas to inspect and for audit questions. a. Arrangements between air traffic control and the apron management unit; b. Clear bifurcation of scope of work for the ATC and the apron management unit;

c. Arrangement for allocating aircraft parking positions; d. Arrangement for initiating engine start and ensuring clearance of aircraft push back; e. Marshalling service; f. Arrangement for follow me services;

g. Arrangement for expeditious movement of vehicles; h. Arrangement for highly coordinated two-way communication between the aircrafts, vehicle, apron control unit and the ATC; i. System and procedures for aircraft and vehicle control in low visibility operating conditions;

j. Procedure for reporting incidents/accidents.

CONCLUSION:

The inspector needs to plan the inspection of this area with attention to all elements of the system including responsibilities, operating procedures, reporting and communication and training as well as seeking assurances about system compliance, the inspector will be keen to see evidence of coordinated and disciplined activity on the maneuvering area during normal and low visibility operations.

Figure 12.21.4.1 – INSPECTION/SURVEILLANCE NOTIFICATION LETTER Figure 12.21.5.1 – ASSESSMENT OF OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE PERSONNEL 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to the aerodrome inspectors while assessing the competency of aerodrome human resource associated with operations and maintenance of aerodrome.

2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137, 138 and 139. 3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspectors when verifying the competency of operational and maintenance staff at the time of onsite inspection for issue of certificate or during introduction of a new facility and associated procedures or during surveillance. The recurrent/refresher training of such personnel should also be evaluated.

4. The inspector should always ensure that the current and updated copy of the checklist is used. 5. The observations, if there are any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the aerodrome operator for action.

The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance. 6. Records of such completed checklists and action taken must be maintained.

CHECKLIST ON ASSESSMENT OF OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE PERSONNEL

Name of Aerodrome: Name of Responsible Post Holder: S.N. ITEM YES NO N/A Does the officer/ employee have a job description? Does the officer/ employee possess basic qualifications to carry out assigned responsibilities? Does the officer/ employee have the required knowledge and experience including on the job training (OJT) to perform the responsibilities at the expected level of competence? Does the officer/ employee have the required tools and equipment to carry out the operation in line with the job specification? Is there a personal roster that indicates satisfactory workload for each officer? Are the officers/ employees adequately and regularly trained to discharge responsibility optimally? In demonstrating operations and maintenance competence, aerodrome movement area, obstacle limitation surface, markings, signs and lights for conducting or supervising aerodrome works for using the portable radio? Whether the officers have undergone refresher trainings at such duration/interval to guarantee currency on the job? Does the officers have adequate knowledge of the working documents available for the performance of his duties? Inspector’s Remark:

Signature: Date: Figure 12.21.6.1 – ASSESSMENT OF COMPETENCY OF RFF PERSONNEL 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to aerodrome inspectors while assessing the competency of RFF personnel employed at the aerodrome.

2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137, 138 and 139. 3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspectors when verifying the competency of RFF personnel at the time of onsite inspection for aerodrome certification process and during the introduction of a new facility and associated procedures. During surveillance inspection, the recurrent/ refresher training along with the training based on the recommendations of various exercises of such personnel should be evaluated.

4. The inspector should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. 5. The observations, if any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action and the action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance.

6. Records of all such completed checklists and action taken must be maintained.

CHECKLIST ON ASSESSING THE COMPETENCE OF RFF PERSONNEL

Name of Aerodrome: Head of Department: S.N. ITEM YES NO N/A Do the RFF personnel have the required qualifications necessary to perform their duties in an efficient manner? Are there sufficiently qualified personnel to meet the requirements? In demonstrations, the RFF personnel competence, the knowledge, skills and experience required? e.g.

response time exercise, don/ doff PPE, operating equipment. Are the RFF personnel properly equipped with necessary tools? Records of training for RFF kept and updated inclusive of Periodic and Proficiency training? Are the certificates obtained by the RFF personnel valid? Are the RFF personnel familiar with and conversant with the standards operating procedure and working documents? Inspector’s Remark:

Signature: Date: Figure 12.21.7.1 – ASSESSMENT OF RFF SERVICES 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to the aerodrome inspector while verifying the availability of RFF services as detailed in the Aerodrome Manual.

2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137, 138 and 139. 3. The checklist must be completed by the Aerodrome Inspector when verifying the availability and adequacy RFF services for the intended type of operations at the time of licensing inspection or its preparedness during the surveillance inspections.

4. The inspectors should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. 5. The availability of human resources, facilities, procedures, training etc. for each function should be verified for effective implementation of requirement referenced at point 2.

6. The observation, if there is any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance. 7. Records of all such completed checklists and action taken must be maintained.

CHECKLIST FOR ASSESSMENT OF RFF SERVICES

The checklist includes general safety items, training and administration, operational, ambulance and medical requirements. Efforts should be made to cover areas depending on the time allocated for the inspection and deficiencies to be affected quoting the references of GACAR and Guidance Material.

Name of Aerodrome: Date of Inspection: Aerodrome category for Rescue and Fire Services: Responsible Post Holder: S.N. Inspection Item Reference Remarks General Requirements A.Level of Protection A.1 Is the level of protection provided at the aerodrome for aircraft rescue and firefighting compliant with the aerodrome category? A.2 Does the aerodrome have a fire station that is located so that the access for rescue and fire fighting vehicles into the runway area is direct and clear, requiring a minimum number of turns? A.3 Can the fire station house all RFF vehicles and provide sufficient space for each vehicle and the surrounding area in which personnel can work conveniently? A.4 Are the amounts of extinguishing agents available comply with the minimum amounts of extinguishing agents required for the aerodrome category? A.5 Is the amount of foam concentration provided on a vehicle sufficient to produce at least two full loads of foam solution? A.6 Is a reserve supply of foam concentrate, equivalent to 200% of the required quantities? A.7 Is a reserve supply of complementary, equivalent to 100% of the required quantities? A.8 Is the response time test for all firefighting vehicles regularly conducted and recorded? A.9 Can firefighting vehicle(s) achieve the response time test to any point of each operational runway and discharge 50% of the amount of the extinguishing agent within response time not exceeding three minutes successfully? B.Aerodrome Facility Affecting Rescue and Firefighting Services B.1 Are the firefighting extinguishers, first aid kit, hose reels and hydrants available on the aerodrome services as required? B.2 Dose the rescue and firefighting services in its facilities observe Human Factors principle to ensure Operational effectiveness of RFF Personnel? C.Communication and Alarm Equipment C.1 Are there any public address and alarm system in the place C.2 Are there any discrete communication systems available for communication with the control tower (if available) any other fire station on the aerodrome and the rescue and fire fighting vehicles? C.3 Are there an alerting system available for rescue and firefighting personnel, capable of being operated from that station, control tower (if available), and any other fire station on the aerodrome (if available)? C.4 Is the quantity of hand radio equipment sufficient to enable communication between vehicle operators, control personnel and rescue operators? C.5 Are all telephones and radio equipment in FCC being regularly tested for serviceability, documents and maintained for the same? D.Rescue and Firefighting Vehicle D.1 Are the required number of serviceable vehicles available as per aerodrome category? D.2 Are all RFF vehicles that are available at the fire station not extending to fifteen years old? D.3 If the international Aerodrome category is (9) or (10), is there a Escape Stair Vehicle available? D.4 Is the record of performance test, the periodicity and the date of the last check carried out available? D.5 Is the regular testing of foam making systems being carried out? Evidence is required D.6 Are there rescue equipment commensurate with the level of aircraft operations and as per ICAO requirement available on RFF vehicle(s)? D.5 Is there a follow-up checklist available to substantiate the serviceability of the equipment on vehicle? D.6 Are there electrical generators for utilization with equipment and lighting? D.7 Does all emergency lighting operate properly? D.8 Is an updated copy of the GRID map of the aerodrome and its immediate vicinity available on all RFF vehicles? E.Protecting Clothing and Respiratory Equipment E.1 Are each responding RFF personnel provided with protective clothing and respiratory equipment to enable them to perform their duties in an effective manner? E.2 Are there enough number of breathing apparatus (BA) sets? E.3 Are all BA sets and cylinders regularly tested? E.4 Is there an adequate arrangement for recharging air cylinders with pure air? F.Personnel Requirement F.1 Has the aerodrome operator conducted Task Resource Analysis (TRA) for determining minimum number of rescue and firefighting personnel level of staffing? F.2 Has the operator documented TRA in the aerodrome manual? F.3 Does the number of firefighting personnel per shift meet the requirement as per TRA? G.Training, Administration and Application of Human Factors G.1 Is there an accepted annual training program for initial, recurrent and the on-job training for RFF personnel? G.2 Are there special or refresher training activities provided in preparation for an infrequently performed operation? G.3 Is fatigue managed to ensure no person is scheduled to perform RFF services for more than 24 consecutive hours of duty each seventy-two (72) hours? G.4 Does the fire station(s) regularly maintained and serviced? G.5 Are the alarms audible above background noise both inside, outside and around the fire station(s)? G.6 Is the training imparted being documented? G.7 Is sufficient information and training equipment available. e.g. Charts of aircraft escape hatches and emergency entrances etc.? G.18 Do firefighting personnel undergo annual physical and medical examinations? Records thereof H.Aerodrome Emergency Exercise H.1 Check if the Aerodrome Emergency Plan accepted by GACA? H.2 Whether procedures exist for operation in low visibility conditions? H.3 Whether full scale aerodrome emergency exercise conducted at intervals as per GACAR.? H.4 Whether the partial emergency exercises in the intervening year to ensure that any deficiency found during the full-scale aerodrome emergency exercise is conducted.? H.5 Whether a fixed emergency operation center and a mobile command post are available for use during an emergency? I.Ambulance and Medical Services I.1 Whether the ambulance and medical facilities are part of the aerodrome operation services? I.2 Whether the ambulance(s) is equipped with sufficient medical supplies ready to be used? I.3 Are the medical supplies on the aerodrome regularly checked for the expiry dates? Inspector’s Remark:

Signature: Date: Figure 12.21.8.1 – ASSESSMENT OF AERODROME OPERATIONAL PROCEDURES 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to aerodrome inspectors while verifying the aerodrome operational services as submitted in Chapter 5 of the Aerodrome Manual.

2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137,138 and 139. 3. The checklist must be completed by the Aerodrome Inspector when verifying the availability and adequacy of aerodrome operational for the intended type of operations at the time of licensing inspection or its preparedness during the surveillance inspections.

4. The inspectors should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. The availability of human resources, facilities, procedures, training etc. for each function should be verified for effective implementation of requirement referenced at point 2.

5. The observation, if there is any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance. 6. Records of all such completed checklists and action taken must be maintained.

CHECK LIST FOR AERODROME OPERATIONAL PROCEDURES.

Name of the Airport: _____________________________________ Name of Accountable Executive: ___________________________ Date of On-Site Verification: _______________________________ S.N. Airport Operational Procedure Yes No N/A Promulgation of Information in AIP Control of Access Emergency Planning Rescue and Firefighting Services Inspection of the Movement Area Maintenance of the Movement Area Snow and Ice Control and Other Hazardous Meteorological Condition Visual Aids Apron Management Apron Safety Management Vehicles on the Movement Area Wildlife Hazard Management Obstacles Removal of a Disabled Aeroplane Dangerous Goods Low Visibility Operations Protection of Sites for Radar, Navigation Aids and Meteorological Equipment Aerodrome Works Safety Inspector’s name:

Signature Date: Figure 12.21.9.1 – ASSESSMENT OF AERODROME EMERGENCY PLAN 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to the aerodrome inspector while verifying the availability and effectiveness of Aerodrome Emergency Plan as included in Chapter 5 of the Aerodrome Manual 2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137,138 139.

3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspectors when verifying the availability and adequacy of AEP at the time of onsite inspection for certification process and also during surveillance inspections. It should also be checked for periodic review of certain contents susceptible to change and for amendments carried out based on feedback/suggestions from training drills and exercises.

4. The inspector should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. The availability of human resources, facilities, procedures, training, etc. for each function should be verified for effective implementation of the requirement referenced at item 2.

5. The observations, if any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance. 6. Records of all such completed checklists and action taken must be maintained.

CHECKLIST FOR ASSESSMENT OF AERODROME EMERGENCY PLAN

Aerodrome Name: Date of Inspection: SI No. Inspection Item Reference Observation Aerodrome Emergency Plan A.1 Is an Aerodrome Emergency Plan established at your aerodrome commensurate with the aircraft operations and other activities conducted aerodrome? A.2 Does the plan provide for the coordination of the actions to be taken in an emergency occurring at an emergency occurring at an aerodrome or in its vicinity? A.3 Does the plan observe Human factors principles to ensure optimum response by all existing agencies participating in emergency operations? A.4 Whether coordination of the actions of various agencies is available? A.5 Is the emergency operations Centre a part of the aerodrome facilities? A.6 Whether grid map of the aerodrome and its immediate vicinity available A.7 Whether Emergency mutual aid agreements between concerned agencies available? A.8 Whether Emergency management center is available? A.9 Is an adequate communication system provided to link the command post and the emergency operations Centre with each other and with the participating agencies in accordance with the plan and consistent with the requirements of the aerodrome? B.1 Fixed Emergency Operations Centre B.1.1 Whether it is equipped with the communication equipment grid map of the aerodrome and its vicinity B.1.2 Whether communication and electronic devices are checked regularly? B.2 Mobile Command Post B.2.1 Whether Mobile Command post can move rapidly to the site? B.2.2 Whether it equipped properly? B.2.3 Whether Communication and electronic devices checked each month? B.3 Updated Grid Map B.3.1 Whether the copy of Grid map is given to all participating agencies? B.3.2 Preferably two maps 1.Indicating confines of aerodrome access roads, location of water supplies, rendezvous points, staging areas etc.

2.Surrounding communities and depicting appropriate medical facilities, access roads, rendezvous points etc. B.4 Emergencies in difficult environments B.4.1 Is there any assessment of area within 1000 m of the runway for aerodrome located in difficult environment.

B.4.2 Equipment and/or coordination with appropriate specialist rescue services. B.5 Communications B.5.1 Equipment (e.g. telephone lines, two-way radios, hot lines) B.5.2 Portable megaphones B.5.3 Periodic testing B.5.4 Complete and current list of inter-agency telephone numbers with all participants B.6 Aerodrome Emergency Services B.6.1 Is there a procedure for developing, implementing and periodically testing these plans B.6.2 Full scale exercise B.6.3 Partial exercise B.6.4 Series of Modular tests B.6.5 Review of Aerodrome Emergency Plan 1.Critique Meeting 2.Observations 3.Recommendations Training and Records (Initial and Recurrent) C.1 Aerodrome familiarization C.2 Aircraft familiarization C.3 Emergency communication including aircraft fire related alarms C.4 Emergency aircraft evacuation assistance C.5 Application of Human Performance principles in emergency plan Signature of Inspector:

Date: Figure 12.21.10.1 – ASSESSMENT OF LOW VISIBILITY PROCEDURES (LVP) 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to aerodrome inspectors, where applicable, to verify that the procedure is developed and implemented for low visibility operations, as included in Chapter 5 of the Aerodrome Manual 2. The reference documents for the checklist is GACAR Part 137,138 and 139.

3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspectors when verifying the availability and adequacy of LVP at the time of licensing process or when aerodrome operator has upgraded the facilities for LVP, it should be assessed during surveillance inspections.

4. The inspector should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. The availability of human resources, facilities, procedures, training, etc. for each function should be verified for effective implementation of requirement referenced at item 2.

5. The observations, if any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance.

Comments/Suggestions: Figure 12.21.13.1 – ASSESSMENT OF MARKING AND LIGHTING OF OBJECTS 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to the aerodrome inspector for verifying that the significant objects inside the aerodrome and in its vicinity are marked and lighted.

2. The reference documents for the checklist is GACAR-139 3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspector at the time of certification/re-certification during surveillance inspections. 4. The inspectors should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used.

5. The observations, if there are any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the aerodrome operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance.

DATE OF INSPECTION:

S.N. Inspection Item Reference Observation A. Obstacle Monitoring A.1 System of monitoring the growth of obstacle in the vicinity of the aerodrome Aerodrome Manual Obstacle Lighting B.1 Low intensity obstacle lights Type-C flashing blue for emergencies or security vehicles and flashing yellow for other vehicles Table I-1 of GACAR

Part 139

B.2 Low intensity lights Type-D light on Follow-Me vehicles Table I-1 of GACAR

Part 139

B.3 Low intensity fixed lights on objects with limited mobility Table I-1 of GACAR

Part 139

B.4 Provision of red light on fixed obstacles of less than 45 m

GACAR 139.603

(c)(6)(i) B.5 Provision of red lights on fixed obstacles of more than 45 m

GACAR 139.603

(c)(7)(iii) B.6 Unserviceable parts of the movement area are delineated by fixed red lights with intensity not less than 10 cd GACAR 139.707 (d) Obstacle Marking C.1 Obstacle outside the lateral boundaries of OLS marked and lighted except that marking be omitted when the high intensity obstacle lights are provided by day GACAR (b)(1) C.2 Whether obstacle by day marked by checkered pattern. (red or orange and white) GACAR 139.601 (c)(2) C.3 Check flags used to mark extensive or closely spaced objects and are displayed at least every 15 m GACAR (c)(3)(i) C.4 Mobile objects are marked by color red or yellowish green for emergency vehicles and yellow for service vehicles.

GACAR (b)(2) C.5 Availability and implementation of procedures for maintenance of obstructions marking and lighting. Aerodrome Manual Name and signature of Inspector: Date: Figure 12.21.14.1 – ASSESSMENT OF VISUAL AIDS 1. The purpose of this checklist is to provide guidance and information to aerodrome inspectors for verifying electric systems for power supply, system design and monitoring of visual aids.

2. The reference documents for the checklist are GACAR Part 137,138 and 139. 3. The checklist must be completed by the aerodrome inspector at the time of initial certification/re-certification, commissioning of new or upgraded facility and during surveillance.

4. The inspector should always ensure that the current updated copy of this checklist is used. 5. The observations, if any, must be recorded in detail and communicated to the operator for action. The action taken must be reviewed for compliance and acceptance.

RESPONSIBLE POST HOLDER:

POWER SUPPLY SYSTEM (SWITCH OVER TIME) (TABLE K-1; GACAR Part 139) Facility Non-Instrument Non-Precision Precision Cat I Precision Cat II/III RVR <800 PAPI Approach Lighting System Runway Edge Runway Threshold Light Runway End Light Essential Taxiways Obstacles Runway Centerline Lights Runway TDZ Lights All Stop Bars

LIGHTING DESIGN CHECKLIST

Check the system to ensure that in case of failure of any equipment, it will not leave the pilot with inadequate visual guidance or misleading information like interleaving of the circuits. GACAR 139.801 (b) Where the secondary power supply of an aerodrome is provided using duplicate feeders, check if such supplies are physically and electrically separate to ensure the required level of availability and independence.

GACAR 139.803 (b) Where a runway forming part of a standard taxi route is provided with runway lighting and taxiway lighting, whether the lighting system is interlocked to preclude the possibility of simultaneous operation of both forms of lighting GACAR 139.803 (c)

CONTROL AND MONITORING SYSTEM

Check the availability and status of the lighting monitoring system GACAR 139.805 (a) Where lighting systems are used for aircraft control purposes and whether such a system is monitored automatically so as to provide an indication of any fault which may affect the control functions. Whether this information is automatically relayed to the air traffic services unit.

GACAR 139.805 (b) Where a change in the operational status of lights has occurred, check if the indication is provided within two seconds for a stop bar at a runway holding position and within five seconds for all other types of visual aids.

GACAR 139.805 (c) Whether the monitoring is automatic for runway is used in less than RVR 550 m conditions to provide indication when the serviceability level of any element falls below the minimum serviceability level specified in GACAR Part 139 as appropriate and check if the information is automatically relayed to the maintenance crew.

GACAR 139.805 (d) Confirm whether the assessment of the visual aids i.e. PAPI, approach lighting system, runway edge and centerline lights etc. has been carried out with flight checks after major maintenance.

Paragraph 5.2, ICAO Doc 9137, Part 8 Check whether the intensity control of the lighting system meets the requirement for the runway intended to be used in minimum conditions of visibility and ambient light.

GACAR Part 139.505 (a)(8) Check if runway centerline lights longitudinal spacing is 30 m or 15 m and runway is intended to be used in RVR conditions above 350 m, if spacing is 30 m confirm that the system is in place to ensure the following serviceability level.

·At least 95 percent of the lights are serviceable in the runway centerline lights and in the runway edge lights; and ·At least 75 percent of the lights are serviceable in the runway end lights. ·An unserviceable light is not allowed adjacent to another unserviceable light.

GACAR 139.1009 (k)

AERODROME MARKINGS

Confirm whether markings are carried out with retro reflective material in case of aerodrome being used for nigh operations. Appendix 3; Paragraph 9, ICAO Doc 9157, Part IV Wherever information marking is provided, check if the provision of an information sign is impractical.

GACAR 139.503 (q)(1)(i) Check the effectiveness of the information markings on complex taxiways. GACAR 139.503 (q)(1)(iii) Inspector: Signature: Date:

12.22.1.1. GACA ACTIVITY REPORT (GAR).

To be developed. 12.22.1.2. OBJECTIVE. This chapter is to be read in conjunction with the following, which all Inspectors must be familiar with: • eBook Volume 12 Chapter 1 General Surveillance/Inspection Policies and Procedures, and • eBook Volume 8 Chapter 8.0 Administration and General This chapter contains additional instructions for Inspectors and provides information regarding the documentation to be used when conducting ANS safety oversight activities (audits or inspections) of certificated ANS providers.

12.22.1.3. INSPECTOR RESPONSIBILITIES. In addition to the provisions contained in the above references, prior to performing a safety oversight activity Inspectors must be familiar, as applicable, with the following:

• Operations Specifications (OpSpecs) of the service provider (including the privileges, authorizations, limitations, specialized services, designated management, approved or accepted manuals, etc.) • Service provider documentation (including current revisions of operations and quality manuals, operating and maintenance instructions, safety management documentation, contingency plans, specific formal arrangements, validation tools, etc.) • Planned and on-going changes (including deployment of new equipment and systems, replacement of existing facilities, nomination of new managers, automation tools, amendments to operating procedures including changes to the QMS, etc.) Additionally, Inspectors must ensure that appropriate coordination with other Inspectors and/or managers within ANS takes place.

12.22.1.4. FORMS. The following Forms are to be used: • ANS Safety Oversight Report (GACA-ANS-SOR) (Figure 12.22.1.1) for interim, final and follow-up safety oversight reports. • Checklist form (Figure 12.22.1.2).

• Safety Oversight Work Plan template (Figure 12.22.1.3). • Finding form (Figure 12.22.1.4). • Assignment of Audit/Inspection Team (Figure 12.22.1.5). 12.22.1.5. TASK OUTCOME. The GACA-ANS-SOR must be completed in accordance with the instructions provided in eBook Volume 8.

The concerned manager must ensure that the GACA-ANS-SOR provides a record that the following have been accomplished: • The safety oversight activity has been conducted in a satisfactory manner. • Concerns and findings have been communicated to the designated focal point or counterpart of the service provider and the management identified in the OpSpecs.

• Follow-up safety oversight activities have been identified for specific concerns or issues. This may involve assessing whether increased surveillance, enforcement measures, or enhanced coordination and follow-up, are required.

• All findings and observations are recorded in the follow-up master file. If enforcement action is necessary, Inspectors must follow the provisions contained in eBook Volume 13, Compliance Enforcement and Resolution of Identified Safety Deficiencies.

Figure 12.22.1.1 – GACA ANS Safety Oversight Report (GACA-ANS-SOR) template

APPENDIX C – FINDING AND OBSERVATION FORMS

** END of Interim or Follow-up Audit Report ** Figure 12.22.1.2 - Checklist form Figure 12.22.1.3 - Safety Oversight Work Plan templat Figure 12.22.1.4 - Finding form Figure 12.22.1.5 - Assignment of Audit/Inspection Team
